name: Oni
description: Oni are fearsome ogre-like demons from Japanese folklore. They are often depicted as having wild hair, sharp claws, and one or more horns growing from their heads. Their skin color varies, with red, blue, and green being the most common. Oni are symbols of chaos, destruction, and untamed power. They are frequently portrayed wielding iron clubs (kanabō) and wearing tiger-skin loincloths. While often malevolent, some stories feature oni who are benevolent protectors or even allies to humans. Their origins are complex, potentially stemming from early Shinto spirits or foreign influences. Oni are deeply ingrained in Japanese culture, appearing in art, literature, and theatrical performances like Noh and Kabuki. They are also associated with Setsubun, a festival where people throw roasted soybeans to drive away bad luck and oni. The image of the oni continues to evolve in modern media, where they are sometimes portrayed as anti-heroes or misunderstood creatures with complex motivations. They are powerful and often terrifying figures, embodying the darker aspects of the human psyche and the untamed forces of nature.
subtitle: Ogre-like demons of Japanese folklore
appearance: Oni typically have a large, muscular build with wild, unkempt hair. Their skin color can range from red, blue, green, or even black. They often possess sharp claws, prominent fangs, and one or more horns on their heads. Many depictions show them wearing tiger-skin loincloths and wielding iron clubs (kanabō). Their facial expressions are usually fierce and intimidating.
habitat: Oni are often said to inhabit remote mountains, forests, caves, or other wild places. They can also be found in the underworld or other supernatural realms. Some stories depict them living in abandoned buildings or preying on unsuspecting travelers.
behavior: Oni are generally depicted as cruel, violent, and malicious beings who enjoy causing chaos and tormenting humans. They are known for their strength, ferocity, and cunning. However, some oni are portrayed as being more neutral or even benevolent, acting as protectors or guardians. Their behavior often depends on the specific story and the individual oni in question.

keyMyths.title: Momotaro the Peach Boy
keyMyths.description: Momotaro, a boy born from a peach, defeats a band of oni on Onigashima (Oni Island) with the help of animal companions. This is a classic tale of good triumphing over evil.

keyMyths.title: Setsubun Bean Throwing
keyMyths.description: During Setsubun, people throw roasted soybeans while chanting "Oni wa soto! Fuku wa uchi!" (Demons out! Fortune in!) to drive away bad luck and evil spirits, including oni.

sources.author: Donald Keene
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Japanese literature containing many stories featuring Oni.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Japanese literature containing many stories featuring Oni.

sources.author: Lafcadio Hearn
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things
sources.description: Collection of Japanese ghost stories and folktales, some of which feature Oni.
sources.text: Collection of Japanese ghost stories and folktales, some of which feature Oni.
